passages.title: Cloze Test 17-21
passages.text: Deep-sea exploration remains one of the most challenging frontiers of modern science. The extreme pressure and cold temperatures  (___)  the depths of the ocean make it difficult for humans to stay for long periods. Therefore, researchers rely  (___)  sophisticated robotic submersibles to collect data. These machines  (___)  to withstand the crushing force of the water while capturing high-resolution images.  (___)  the difficulty of the mission, scientists have discovered numerous new species. This research is  (___)  for understanding the biodiversity of our planet.

passages.title: Cloze Test 22-26
passages.text: The rise of social media has fundamentally  (___)  how we communicate and consume information. While it offers a platform for global connection, it also poses risks  (___)  misinformation and echo chambers. Users often find  (___)  exposed to a narrow range of viewpoints. This phenomenon can lead to increased polarization  (___)  society.  (___)  , digital literacy is becoming an essential skill in the modern age.

passages.title: Reading 43
passages.text: The proliferation of digital surveillance technologies has fundamentally altered the paradigm of urban privacy. Cities once offered a degree of anonymity through their sheer scale and the fleeting nature of social interactions. However, the integration of facial recognition, ubiquitous CCTV, and the 'Internet of Things' has rendered the public sphere a zone of constant data harvesting. Proponents argue that such measures are essential for public safety and the efficient management of municipal resources, while critics warn of a 'chilling effect' on democratic expression and the erosion of individual autonomy. It is no longer possible to walk down a street without leaving a digital footprint that can be scrutinized by state or corporate actors.

passages.title: Reading 51
passages.text: The Industrial Revolution was not merely a period of technological advancement; it was a fundamental reorganization of human society. The transition from agrarian economies to industrial ones led to unprecedented urbanization, as workers migrated to cities to find employment in factories. This shift brought about significant improvements in productivity but also resulted in dire living conditions for the working class. Overcrowded tenements, lack of sanitation, and long working hours became the norm. Consequently, the 19th century saw the rise of labor unions and social reform movements aimed at mitigating the harsh realities of industrial life. These movements eventually paved the way for modern labor laws and the concept of the welfare state.

questions.question: Passage 2: Bioluminescence, the production of light by living organisms, is a widespread phenomenon in the deep ocean, where sunlight cannot penetrate. This biological light serves various functions, including attracting prey, deterring predators, and communicating with potential mates. For instance, the anglerfish uses a glowing lure to entice smaller fish, while certain jellyfish release a burst of light to startle attackers. Interestingly, the chemical reaction involved typically requires two components: a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. This process is remarkably efficient, producing light with almost no heat loss, unlike human-made light sources. Why is bioluminescence particularly common in the deep ocean?

options.A: Because there are more chemical reactions in cold water.
options.B: Because the lack of sunlight makes biological light advantageous.
options.C: Because deep-sea organisms have larger brains for communication.
options.D: Because humans cannot observe these creatures in their habitat.
options.E: Because the high pressure of the ocean triggers luciferase.

questions.question: Passage 2 (Cont.): According to the text, how does the anglerfish utilize bioluminescence?

options.A: To communicate with other species of fish.
options.B: To frighten away potential predators.
options.C: To lure prey toward its mouth.
options.D: To generate heat in the cold ocean depths.
options.E: To illuminate the path for its mates.

questions.question: Passage 2 (Cont.): What is the role of luciferase in the light-producing process?

options.A: It acts as the primary light-emitting molecule.
options.B: It absorbs heat to prevent the organism from burning.
options.C: It functions as an enzyme that facilitates the reaction.
options.D: It is a predator-deterrent chemical released by jellyfish.
options.E: It converts sunlight into chemical energy for the fish.

questions.question: Passage 2 (Cont.): One notable characteristic of bioluminescent light compared to artificial light is that:

options.A: It is much brighter and can be seen from miles away.
options.B: It requires constant exposure to sunlight to function.
options.C: It is produced with minimal heat energy loss.
options.D: It can only be produced by vertebrate animals.
options.E: It is less efficient than modern LED technology.

options.C: The government's mandate to relocate citizens to cities.
options.D: The discovery of new agricultural techniques in the countryside.
options.E: The decline of labor unions in small villages.

questions.question: Based on the passage, the living conditions of the working class were:

options.A: Improved by the increase in industrial productivity.
options.B: Characterized by adequate housing and short working hours.
options.C: Extremely poor due to overcrowding and lack of hygiene.
options.D: Better than those in the agrarian economies.

options.A: A return to agrarian lifestyles and subsistence farming.
options.B: The total abandonment of technological innovation.
options.C: The emergence of labor unions and social reforms.
options.D: The immediate implementation of the welfare state.
options.E: A decrease in the productivity of urban factories.

options.A: Were developed independently of the Industrial Revolution.
options.B: Have their roots in 19th-century social reform movements.
options.C: Are no longer necessary in today's global economy.
options.D: Were first introduced by factory owners to boost morale.
options.E: Focused primarily on increasing industrial output.

questions.question: Passage 4: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. For a long time, it was believed that the brain's structure was relatively fixed after a certain age. However, research has demonstrated that the brain is dynamic and can change in response to learning, experience, and even injury. For example, when a person learns a new skill, such as playing an instrument, specific areas of the brain associated with that skill grow and become more efficient. Similarly, in cases of brain damage, other parts of the brain can sometimes compensate for the lost functions. This discovery has profound implications for rehabilitation and education, suggesting that the potential for cognitive growth is lifelong. What was the traditional view regarding the brain's structure?

options.A: That it was constantly changing throughout adulthood.
options.B: That it was fixed and unchangeable after a specific age.
options.C: That it could only be altered through surgical intervention.
options.D: That it was entirely dependent on genetic factors.
options.E: That only children possessed the ability to learn new skills.

questions.question: Passage 4 (Cont.): According to the passage, neuroplasticity allows the brain to:

options.A: Recover from any injury without any lasting effects.
options.B: Replace all damaged neurons with new ones immediately.
options.C: Reorganize itself by creating new neural connections.
options.D: Increase its physical size significantly every time a skill is learned.
options.E: Function without the need for oxygen or glucose.

questions.question: Passage 4 (Cont.): The example of playing an instrument is used to illustrate:

options.A: How certain skills are more difficult to learn than others.
options.B: The physical growth of brain areas related to a learned skill.
options.C: The limitations of neuroplasticity in adult learners.
options.D: The superiority of musical training over other forms of education.
options.E: The role of genetics in determining musical talent.

questions.question: Passage 4 (Cont.): What is the significance of neuroplasticity for rehabilitation?

options.A: It means that brain damage is always permanent and irreversible.
options.B: It proves that only physical therapy can help recover lost functions.
options.C: It suggests that other parts of the brain can take over for damaged areas.
options.D: It indicates that rehabilitation is only effective for young children.
options.E: It eliminates the need for medical treatment after a stroke.

questions.question: Passage 5: The concept of 'sustainable development' seeks to balance economic growth with environmental protection and social equity. This approach recognizes that the Earth's resources are finite and that current consumption patterns may jeopardize the well-being of future generations. One key pillar of sustainability is the transition to renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Additionally, promoting circular economies—where waste is minimized and materials are recycled—is crucial. However, achieving global sustainability requires unprecedented cooperation between nations, as environmental issues like climate change do not respect political borders. Critics often argue that the transition is too costly, but proponents maintain that the long-term costs of inaction are far greater. What is a fundamental premise of sustainable development?

options.A: Economic growth should always take precedence over the environment.
options.B: The Earth's resources are infinite and can be exploited indefinitely.
options.C: Present needs must be met without compromising the future's ability to do the same.
options.D: Social equity is less important than technological innovation.
options.E: Recycling is a secondary concern in a circular economy.

questions.question: Passage 5 (Cont.): According to the passage, why is international cooperation necessary?

options.A: To ensure that all nations use the same currency for energy.
options.B: Because environmental problems are not confined by national boundaries.
options.C: To prevent some countries from becoming too economically powerful.
options.D: Because only a few nations possess renewable energy technology.
options.E: To establish a single world government to manage resources.

questions.question: Passage 5 (Cont.): What does a 'circular economy' aim to achieve?

options.A: A continuous cycle of economic inflation and deflation.
options.B: The concentration of wealth within a specific group of people.
options.C: The reduction of waste through the recycling of materials.
options.D: The total reliance on traditional fossil fuels for production.
options.E: A system where goods are produced only for local consumption.

questions.question: Passage 5 (Cont.): How do proponents of sustainability respond to the claim that the transition is too expensive?

options.A: By agreeing that the costs are indeed prohibitive for most nations.
options.B: By suggesting that future generations should bear the financial burden.
options.C: By arguing that the costs of doing nothing will be significantly higher.
options.D: By proposing that social equity should be ignored to save money.
options.E: By advocating for a return to pre-industrial economic models.

questions.question:
James: Have you seen the latest report on the declining biodiversity in tropical rainforests?
Sarah: I have. It's alarming how quickly we are losing species that haven't even been studied yet.
James: Exactly. And the loss isn't just about the animals; it affects the entire global climate system.
Sarah: ----
James: That's true. Without those forests, we lose a critical carbon sink, which accelerates global warming.

options.A: I think the report is slightly exaggerating the situation for impact.
options.B: Rainforests are often called the 'lungs of the planet' for a good reason.
options.C: Many people don't realize that biodiversity is only a local issue.
options.D: We should focus more on urban development than on remote forests.
options.E: The report also mentions that reforestation efforts are finally succeeding.

questions.question:
Professor: Your thesis on the impact of microplastics is quite compelling, but you need more empirical data.
Student: I understand, Professor. I'm currently struggling to find recent studies on deep-sea samples.
Professor: ----
Student: That would be incredibly helpful. I'll reach out to him right away.

options.A: You should probably change your topic to something easier to research.
options.B: The library has several old books on the history of plastic production.
options.C: I could put you in touch with a colleague who is conducting that exact research.
options.D: Microplastics are not as dangerous as the media makes them out to be.
options.E: Have you considered looking at freshwater samples instead?

questions.question:
Interviewer: How do you think AI will change the landscape of the creative industries?
Expert: It's a double-edged sword. On one hand, it automates mundane tasks, allowing for more creative freedom.
Interviewer: And on the other hand?
Expert: ----

options.A: It will completely replace the need for human creativity by 2030.
options.B: The technology is still too primitive to have any real impact.
options.C: There are significant concerns regarding intellectual property and original thought.
options.D: AI is only useful for data analysis, not for anything artistic.
options.E: Most artists are excited about the prospect of being replaced.

questions.question:
Patient: I've been feeling very lethargic lately, despite getting enough sleep.
Doctor: It could be a number of things. Have you noticed any changes in your diet or stress levels?
Patient: My job has been quite demanding lately, and I've been skipping meals.
Doctor: ----

options.A: That sounds like a perfect routine for maintaining high energy levels.
options.B: It's likely that your fatigue is linked to nutritional deficiencies and stress.
options.C: I'll prescribe some sleeping pills to help you rest better at night.
options.D: You should try to work even harder to distract yourself from the tiredness.
options.E: Lethargy is a common symptom of getting too much exercise.

questions.question:
Tourist: Excuse me, is this the right way to the National Museum of History?
Local: You're heading in the right direction, but it's quite a long walk from here.
Tourist: Oh, I see. Is there a faster way to get there?
Local: ----

options.A: Walking is the best way to see the city's hidden gems.
options.B: The museum is actually closed for renovations this month.
options.C: You can take the subway from the next station; it's only two stops away.
options.D: I'm not sure where the museum is, actually.
options.E: The National Museum is located in a different city altogether.

questions.question: Hardly had the government announced the new tax policy when a wave of protests erupted across the country.

options.A: The government announced the tax policy after the protests had already started.
options.B: As soon as the new tax policy was made public, people throughout the nation began to protest.
options.C: The protests were so intense that the government was forced to announce a new tax policy.
options.D: The government expected protests, so they delayed the announcement of the tax policy.
options.E: There were almost no protests following the government's announcement of the new tax policy.

questions.question: It wasn't until the end of the 19th century that scientists began to understand the true nature of atoms.

options.A: Scientists understood atoms perfectly well before the 19th century ended.
options.B: The nature of atoms was discovered at the beginning of the 19th century.
options.C: By the end of the 19th century, scientists had lost interest in studying atoms.
options.D: Before the late 1800s, the real characteristics of atoms were not yet grasped by scientists.
options.E: Only a few scientists understood atoms until the 20th century began.

questions.question: If the team had prepared more thoroughly, they wouldn't have faced such a humiliating defeat in the finals.

options.A: The team prepared as much as they could, but they still lost the final match.
options.B: Despite their thorough preparation, the team was defeated in the finals.
options.C: The team's embarrassing loss in the finals was a result of their insufficient preparation.
options.D: The team won the finals because they had prepared more than their opponents.
options.E: Because they were so well-prepared, the team avoided a defeat in the final match.

questions.question: The company's decision to downsize was prompted by a significant decline in international sales.

options.A: International sales dropped, which led the company to decide to reduce its workforce.
options.B: The company decided to expand its operations because international sales were increasing.
options.C: Despite a rise in sales, the company chose to downsize for other reasons.
options.D: If the company hadn't downsized, their international sales would have decreased further.
options.E: The decline in sales was a direct result of the company's decision to downsize.

questions.question: The concept of the 'uncanny valley' describes a phenomenon where human-like figures that are almost, but not quite, perfectly realistic trigger feelings of unease or revulsion. ----. This dip in emotional response occurs between a 'barely human' robot and a 'fully human' person. Researchers suggest that this reaction may be rooted in an evolutionary drive to avoid pathogens or a subconscious fear of the 'non-living' appearing to be alive.

options.A: Robots that look clearly mechanical are often perceived as cute or friendly.
options.B: This is why animated films always strive for 100% realism in their characters.
options.C: However, once the figure becomes indistinguishable from a human, the revulsion disappears.
options.D: The term was first coined by a Japanese roboticist in the 1970s.
options.E: Most people prefer robots that do not look like humans at all.

questions.question: Economic globalization has led to increased interconnectedness between nations, facilitating the flow of goods, services, and capital. ----. While some countries have experienced rapid growth, others have struggled to compete, leading to greater wealth gaps. This disparity has fueled political movements that advocate for protectionism and a return to more localized economies.

options.A: The benefits of this process have been distributed evenly across the globe.
options.B: However, this integration has also resulted in significant economic inequality.
options.C: International trade agreements are the primary reason for this success.
options.D: Globalization has primarily benefited the environment through shared technology.
options.E: There is no longer any need for national borders in the modern economy.

questions.question: In literature, the 'anti-hero' is a protagonist who lacks traditional heroic qualities, such as idealism or courage. ----. These characters often operate in a moral gray area, making choices that are selfish or even unethical. Despite their flaws, they often resonate with audiences because their struggles feel more authentic and relatable than those of a perfect hero.

options.A: They are always the villains of the story who try to destroy the world.
options.B: Instead, they may be motivated by self-interest or a cynical worldview.
options.C: The anti-hero is a relatively new concept that emerged in the 21st century.
options.D: Heroes are usually depicted as having no weaknesses or internal conflicts.
options.E: Modern readers prefer characters who always do the right thing.

questions.question: Renewable energy sources are becoming increasingly cost-effective compared to fossil fuels. ----. For instance, solar and wind power depend on weather conditions, which means energy production can be inconsistent. To address this, significant investments are being made in battery storage technology to ensure a steady supply of electricity even when the sun isn't shining.

options.A: This has led to a total abandonment of coal and gas power plants.
options.B: Governments are no longer subsidizing renewable energy projects.
options.C: However, the issue of intermittency remains a major challenge.
options.D: The cost of solar panels has actually increased over the last decade.
options.E: Fossil fuels are still the most efficient way to generate electricity.

questions.question: (I) The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes that connected the East and West. (II) It was central to the cultural interaction between regions for many centuries. (V) This cross-cultural blending significantly influenced the development of civilizations in Asia and Europe.

options.C: III
options.D: IV

questions.question: (I) Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants transform light energy into chemical energy. (II) During this process, plants take in carbon dioxide and release oxygen as a byproduct. (III) This is why forests are often referred to as the earth's lungs. (IV) Most plants have roots that absorb water and minerals from the soil. (V) This oxygen production is vital for the survival of almost all aerobic organisms on Earth.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that does not emit or interact with electromagnetic radiation. (II) It is estimated to make up about 85% of the matter in the universe. (III) Astronomers use telescopes to observe the visible light from distant stars. (IV) Its existence is inferred from gravitational effects that cannot be explained by visible matter. (V) Understanding dark matter is one of the most significant challenges in modern cosmology.

options.A: I

options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Urban agriculture involves growing plants and raising animals within city environments. (II) It can help improve food security in low-income urban neighborhoods. (III) High-rise buildings are increasingly common in densely populated cities. (IV) Furthermore, it reduces the carbon footprint associated with transporting food from rural farms. (V) Community gardens also foster social cohesion among diverse groups of residents.
